title: State building: Roman empire
description: Answer questions about the Roman empire based on a passage.
topic_path: From Roman Republic to Roman Empire

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following passage.


> "After the establishment of one-man rule at the end of the first century BCE, for more than two hundred years there is no significant history of change at Rome. Autocracy represented, in a sense, an end of history. Of course there were all kind of events, battles, assassinations, political stand-offs, new initiatives and inventions; and the participants would have have all kinds of exciting stories to tell and disputes to argue. But unlike the story of the development of the Republic and the growth of imperial power, which revolutionised almost every aspect of the world of Rome, there was no fundamental change in the structure of Roman politics, empire, or society between the end of the first century BCE and the end of the second century CE."

From Mary Beard, _SPQR: A History of Ancient Rome_ (New York: Liveright Publishing Corporation, 2015), 335-336. 



**What is Beard’s claim about how the creation of the Roman Empire affected continuity and change in Rome?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Beard doesn't claim that both periods were socially stable.

choices.content: The late Republic was a period of revolutionary political and social change; the early empire was a period of political and social stability
The late Republic was a period of political and social stability; the early empire was a period of revolutionary political and social change
Both the late Republic and early empire were periods of political and social stability
Both the late Republic and the early empire were periods of revolutionary political and social change

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following passage.

> "After the establishment of one-man rule at the end of the first century BCE, for more than two hundred years there is no significant history of change at Rome. Autocracy represented, in a sense, an end of history. Of course there were all kind of events, battles, assassinations, political stand-offs, new initiatives and inventions; and the participants would have have all kinds of exciting stories to tell and disputes to argue. But unlike the story of the development of the Republic and the growth of imperial power, which revolutionised almost every aspect of the world of Rome, there was no fundamental change in the structure of Roman politics, empire, or society between the end of the first century BCE and the end of the second century CE."

From Mary Beard, _SPQR: A History of Ancient Rome_ (New York: Liveright Publishing Corporation, 2015), 335-336. 


**Which of the following evidence supports this claim?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Evidence doesn't show that autocratic governments always produce stable political environments.

choices.content: There continued to be internal events and disputes in Rome
The political structures Augustus created changed very little over two centuries
There were many events and conflicts that changed the structures of the Republic in the first century BCE

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following passage.

> "After the establishment of one-man rule at the end of the first century BCE, for more than two hundred years there is no significant history of change at Rome. Autocracy represented, in a sense, an end of history. Of course there were all kind of events, battles, assassinations, political stand-offs, new initiatives and inventions; and the participants would have have all kinds of exciting stories to tell and disputes to argue. But unlike the story of the development of the Republic and the growth of imperial power, which revolutionised almost every aspect of the world of Rome, there was no fundamental change in the structure of Roman politics, empire, or society between the end of the first century BCE and the end of the second century CE."

From Mary Beard, _SPQR: A History of Ancient Rome_ (New York: Liveright Publishing Corporation, 2015), 335-336. 


**Another historian might best _challenge_ Beard’s argument by showing that** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A historian wouldn't be able to challenge Beard's argument by saying there were no new events, battles, etc., because the historical record shows these things did happen under the Roman empire autocracy.

choices.content: social structures in the first century CE were very different than in the second century CE
the same political offices that existed under  Augustus still existed at the end of the second century CE
the power of Roman emperors was not really absolute
there were no “events, battles, assassinations, political standoffs, new initiatives and inventions”
